planting.note: Sow seeds in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seeds require extended chilling near freezing to break dormancy and will germinate the following spring.

directions: Collect ripe fruits in autumn and extract seeds, then sow directly in a prepared outdoor seedbed or in deep pots left outside to receive natural winter chilling. Seeds require 90–180 days near freezing to break dormancy and will not germinate reliably without this cold period. Transplant seedlings to their permanent location in well-drained soil once they reach 6–12 inches tall, ideally in their second or third year. Patience is essential: trees take 7–15 years to bear their first crop of small, astringent sorbets, which soften and sweeten after a frost or brief bletting period.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall directly in a sheltered seedbed; the cold of winter provides the stratification needed for spring germination.

directions: Harvest berries in early autumn and mash to separate seeds from pulp, then rinse seeds thoroughly before sowing to remove germination inhibitors in the flesh. Sow in a prepared seedbed or deep containers left outdoors to experience natural winter cold, which substitutes for manual stratification. Swedish Whitebeam is exceptionally adaptable and tolerates coastal winds, compacted urban soils, and exposed hillsides better than most ornamental trees. Move seedlings to their permanent site by the second year, choosing a location with full sun and good drainage where the tree can develop its distinctive white-backed foliage.

planting.note: Sow fresh seeds in fall immediately after harvest; cold winter temperatures provide the natural stratification required for germination the following spring.

directions: Collect ripe blue-black berries in midsummer and sow seeds fresh in fall, pressing them lightly into moist, humus-rich soil in a sheltered outdoor bed or cold frame. Unlike most serviceberries that bear fruits in long clusters, Bartram's Serviceberry ripens single fruits or small groups of 2–4, making hand-harvest easy. This cold-hardy species thrives in cool, moist conditions resembling its native boreal and subalpine habitat; avoid planting in heavy clay or hot, dry sites. Established shrubs are trouble-free and begin producing their first meaningful berry crops within 2–5 years of transplanting.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall into well-drained, sandy or rocky soil; the shrub's natural habitat preference makes it well suited to difficult sites where improved soils are unavailable.

directions: Sow fresh seeds in fall directly into sandy, well-drained soil, as Low Serviceberry is adapted to dry, rocky ground and performs poorly in rich or consistently moist conditions. Winter cold provides the stratification seeds need, and seedlings emerge in spring when soil temperatures warm to 40–55 degrees Fahrenheit. This spreading, colony-forming shrub is excellent for naturalizing slopes, stabilizing sandy soils, and providing wildlife habitat; allow adequate space for root-sprouting colonies to develop. Expect the first berry crop 2–5 years after transplanting; the sweet, dark blue fruits ripen in midsummer and are relished by birds and mammals.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall on rocky or sandy, well-drained soil; this species naturally colonizes exposed outcrops and barrens where soil is thin and lean.

directions: Harvest berries when fully dark blue-black and extract seeds by mashing and rinsing before sowing in fall into lean, well-drained soil in a sunny outdoor bed. The round, coarsely toothed leaves that give this species its name are a useful identification feature as seedlings mature through their first growing seasons. Roundleaf Serviceberry tolerates shallow, rocky soils and gravelly barrens far better than most shrubs, making it a strong candidate for challenging landscape sites. Plants begin bearing their sweet, dark berries within 2–5 years of transplanting and attract a wide variety of frugivorous birds during the midsummer ripening period.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall in a well-drained seedbed; this vigorous suckering shrub establishes readily from seed and will spread by root sprouts once established.

directions: Sow seeds in fall directly into a prepared bed with lean, well-drained soil, pressing them just below the surface and allowing winter cold to provide the stratification needed for spring germination. Dwarf Serviceberry spreads aggressively by root sprouts and will form dense thickets over time, so site it where naturalized spread is welcome rather than in manicured borders. The profuse white spring flowers provide early nectar for pollinators, and the sweet blue-black berries that follow are among the first wild fruits to ripen each summer. Established colonies are highly drought-tolerant and require virtually no maintenance, making this shrub an outstanding choice for habitat restoration, erosion control, or low-maintenance edible landscaping.
